stem: A 14-month-old girl is brought to a regional emergency department 8 minutes after eating her first taste of peanut butter at home. Her mother describes immediate facial flushing and lip swelling, then audible wheeze and one episode of vomiting in the car. The child has eczema and a family history of asthma but no previous food reaction. She weighs 10 kg. On arrival she is distressed, has generalised urticaria, peri-oral oedema, and audible expiratory wheeze with mild intercostal recession. She is alert and crying.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 44, SpO2 94% room air, BP 80/45, Temp 36.8
data_table.Examination: Generalised urticaria; peri-oral oedema without tongue swelling; bilateral wheeze; CRT 3s; alert
data_table.Investigations: Not performed; allergy testing arranged out-patient after stabilisation

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Oral cetirizine 5 mg with planned clinical reassessment for any escalation in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Oral antihistamines treat cutaneous symptoms only and have no role as primary treatment in anaphylaxis; their use risks delaying adrenaline in a patient with airway and circulatory involvement. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg as a bolus while preparing nebulised salbutamol therapy
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have no demonstrated effect on the acute phase of anaphylaxis and do not reliably prevent biphasic reactions; their administration must not precede intramuscular adrenaline. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 0.15 mg of 1:1000 into the anterolateral mid-thigh and reassess promptly
options.rationale: Intramuscular adrenaline 10 micrograms/kg up to 0.5 mg into the anterolateral thigh is the immediate treatment in paediatric anaphylaxis; a 10 to 20 kg child receives 0.15 mg, with repeat doses every 5 minutes if no response. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Subcutaneous adrenaline 0.15 mg with a concurrent intravenous bolus of 20 mL/kg normal saline
options.rationale: Subcutaneous adrenaline is no longer used in anaphylaxis because absorption is slow and unpredictable when peripheral vasoconstriction has occurred; intramuscular delivery into the thigh is essential. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg via mask with oxygen 6 L/min before any parenteral therapy
options.rationale: Nebulised bronchodilators are adjuncts for residual bronchospasm after adrenaline but must never be primary treatment when angioedema, urticaria, and circulatory features confirm systemic anaphylaxis. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

explanation.summary: Paediatric anaphylaxis requires immediate intramuscular adrenaline at 10 micrograms/kg, drawn from a 1:1000 ampoule and delivered into the anterolateral mid-thigh through clothing if needed. For a 10 to 20 kg child the standard dose is 0.15 mg, escalating to 0.3 mg above 20 kg and 0.5 mg in adolescents and adults. Antihistamines, corticosteroids, and bronchodilators are second-line adjuncts only. After the first dose the child should remain supine with the legs elevated if hypotensive; the dose can be repeated every 5 minutes for ongoing or recurrent symptoms.
key_points: Intramuscular adrenaline 10 micrograms/kg is first-line in paediatric anaphylaxis
Subcutaneous and intravenous routes are NOT the standard initial route
Antihistamines and steroids do not treat airway or circulatory compromise
Repeat IM adrenaline every 5 minutes if no clinical response
explanation.pearls: For a 10 to 20 kg paediatric patient the ASCIA IM adrenaline dose is 0.15 mg from a 1:1000 ampoule into the anterolateral thigh, repeated every 5 minutes as needed.

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 is brought to the birth suite at 36+2 weeks gestation reporting a small painless gush of fresh red vaginal blood that began shortly after spontaneous rupture of membranes 20 minutes ago. Her morphology scan at 20 weeks reported a succenturiate placental lobe with the main placenta posterior, no praevia. She is not contracting. The midwife places her on continuous CTG which shows a sinusoidal baseline at 130 bpm with no accelerations. The presenting part is high. She is otherwise well, with no history of trauma.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 96, BP 118/72, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.8
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen; presenting part high; no contractions palpable; small amount of fresh red blood at introitus
data_table.Investigations: Bedside USS: posterior placenta with succenturiate lobe; CTG: sinusoidal pattern baseline 130 bpm

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Augment labour with controlled artificial rupture and an oxytocin infusion targeting active phase
options.rationale: Augmentation in suspected vasa praevia accelerates exsanguination of foetal blood through ruptured vessels; foetal mortality without immediate delivery exceeds 50 percent. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa Praevia)

options.text: Proceed immediately to category 1 caesarean section with neonatal team in attendance for resuscitation
options.rationale: A sinusoidal CTG with fresh PV bleeding after membrane rupture in a woman with known succenturiate lobe is vasa praevia until proven otherwise; foetal blood loss is rapid and immediate category 1 caesarean is the only intervention that prevents foetal death. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa Praevia)

options.text: Perform a careful digital vaginal examination first to assess cervical dilatation and exclude cord prolapse
options.rationale: Digital examination risks further laceration of the vasa praevia vessels and adds no useful information when a category 1 caesarean is already indicated by the sinusoidal CTG. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa Praevia)

options.text: Commence intravenous nifedipine tocolysis and a single dose of betamethasone before planning delivery
options.rationale: Tocolysis and antenatal corticosteroids assume time to delay delivery; in active vasa praevia haemorrhage foetal demise occurs within minutes and steroid course timing is irrelevant. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa Praevia)

options.text: Start intravenous magnesium sulfate for foetal neuroprotection and arrange repeat ultrasound now
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate neuroprotection is appropriate before preterm delivery under 30 weeks gestation; at 36 weeks it is not indicated and does not address the imminent foetal exsanguination. (Source: RANZCOG: Magnesium Sulfate for Neuroprotection)

explanation.summary: Vasa praevia is the presence of unprotected foetal vessels running through the membranes near the cervical os, classically associated with a succenturiate or bilobed placenta or with a velamentous cord insertion. Rupture of membranes shears these vessels and causes rapid foetal exsanguination, presenting as painless fresh PV bleeding with a sinusoidal CTG within minutes. The intervention is immediate category 1 caesarean section; any delay for examination, tocolysis, or imaging risks foetal death. Antenatally diagnosed vasa praevia is delivered electively at 34 to 36 weeks after antenatal corticosteroids.
key_points: Painless PV bleed plus sinusoidal CTG after SROM with a known succenturiate lobe is vasa praevia
Foetal blood volume is small so exsanguination occurs within minutes
Treatment is category 1 caesarean - not vaginal delivery or tocolysis
Antenatal diagnosis allows elective caesarean at 34 to 36 weeks after steroids
explanation.pearls: Sinusoidal CTG plus fresh PV bleed plus succenturiate lobe equals vasa praevia equals category 1 caesarean. Vaginal examination only worsens the bleed.

stem: A 28-year-old man presents to the sexual health clinic with 7 days of fever, sore throat, generalised lymphadenopathy, and a non-pruritic maculopapular rash on the trunk. He reports condomless receptive anal intercourse with a new male partner 3 weeks ago and is otherwise well. He has not previously tested for HIV. A rapid third-generation HIV antibody test at the clinic is negative. He asks whether this means he does not have HIV.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96, BP 122/76, SpO2 98% room air, Temp 38.4
data_table.Examination: Tonsillar enlargement without exudate; cervical and inguinal lymphadenopathy; non-pruritic maculopapular rash on the trunk and arms
data_table.Investigations: Rapid third-generation HIV antibody: negative. Other testing pending.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation to clarify his HIV status?

options.text: Repeat the third-generation antibody test in 12 weeks and reassure him in the interim
options.rationale: A 12 week wait misses the window in which acute infection is most transmissible; third-generation antibody only assays can take 6 to 12 weeks to become positive and are not appropriate during a syndrome consistent with seroconversion. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Policy)

options.text: Serum cytomegalovirus IgM and Epstein-Barr virus serology as the leading differential
options.rationale: EBV and CMV are differentials for the mononucleosis-like syndrome but in a man with documented high-risk exposure 3 weeks earlier, prioritising another diagnosis risks missing an acutely treatable HIV infection. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Policy)

options.text: Commence post-exposure prophylaxis with tenofovir-emtricitabine-dolutegravir for 28 days now
options.rationale: Post-exposure prophylaxis is effective only when started within 72 hours of exposure; at 3 weeks the window has closed and the priority is diagnostic confirmation, not prevention. (Source: ASHM Post-exposure Prophylaxis Guidelines)

options.text: Send a fourth-generation HIV antigen and antibody combination assay and a plasma HIV-1 RNA viral load
options.rationale: Acute HIV seroconversion is detected earliest by HIV-1 RNA from approximately day 10 and by fourth-generation Ag/Ab combination assay from approximately day 14 to 21; both should be sent when a third-generation antibody-only test is negative in a patient with a seroconversion syndrome. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Policy)

options.text: Send a syphilis treponemal serology and ceftriaxone for presumptive disseminated gonococcal infection
options.rationale: Other sexually transmitted infections do warrant screening alongside HIV evaluation, but presumptive treatment for disseminated gonococcal infection does not address the central diagnostic question of HIV seroconversion. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Acute HIV seroconversion presents with a non-specific viral illness 1 to 4 weeks after exposure, characteristically fever, sore throat, lymphadenopathy, and a maculopapular trunk rash. Third-generation antibody only assays may remain negative for 6 to 12 weeks. The earliest detectable marker is plasma HIV-1 RNA viral load from approximately day 10; fourth-generation assays detect p24 antigen plus antibody from approximately day 14 to 21. A negative rapid antibody test in a patient with a seroconversion syndrome must be supplemented by HIV RNA and fourth-generation Ag/Ab combination testing. Identifying acute HIV allows immediate antiretroviral therapy, which both improves long-term outcomes and dramatically reduces transmission risk.
key_points: Acute HIV syndrome appears 1 to 4 weeks after exposure
Third-generation antibody alone can be negative for up to 12 weeks
HIV RNA viral load is positive from approximately day 10
Fourth-generation Ag/Ab combination is positive from approximately day 14 to 21
explanation.pearls: When the rapid antibody test is negative but the clinical picture screams seroconversion, the diagnostic pair is HIV RNA plus fourth-generation Ag/Ab combo - do not wait 12 weeks.

stem:
An 18-month-old girl is referred to a paediatric clinic by her general practitioner because her weight has crossed from the 50th centile at 6 months to below the 10th centile, while her height and head circumference have remained on the 25th centile. She walks well, says 10 words, and has reached her 18-month milestones. Her appetite has decreased and she now refuses bread, cereal, and pasta after several episodes of bloating.

Stools are bulky and pale 3 to 4 times per day. There is no history of vomiting, diarrhoeal illness, or recurrent infection. The family is well housed and food secure.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 122, RR 28, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.8
data_table.Examination: Thin child with mild abdominal distension; muscle bulk reduced in buttocks and thighs; no organomegaly; no rash; no oedema
data_table.Investigations: Growth chart: weight crossed two major centiles down; height and head on track

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate first-line investigation panel?

options.text: Coeliac screen with tissue transglutaminase IgA plus total IgA, FBC, ferritin, UEC, TSH, and urine culture
options.rationale: An 18 month old with weight faltering, bulky pale stools, and food avoidance after gluten introduction requires coeliac screening with tissue transglutaminase IgA and total IgA to exclude selective IgA deficiency, alongside baseline FBC, ferritin, UEC, TSH, and urine culture to capture the common organic causes. (Source: RCH CPG: Faltering Growth)

options.text: Bone age radiograph, insulin-like growth factor 1, growth hormone provocation testing, and karyotype
options.rationale: Endocrine investigations are reserved for short stature with preserved weight or proportionate growth failure; in weight-led faltering growth with normal height and head, endocrine workup is low yield and delays nutrient-deficiency identification. (Source: RCH CPG: Faltering Growth)

options.text: Urgent magnetic resonance imaging of the brain to exclude an intracranial space-occupying lesion
options.rationale: Neuroimaging is indicated when head circumference is abnormal or growth failure is paired with neurological signs; isolated weight crossing centiles with preserved head circumference does not warrant MRI. (Source: RCH CPG: Faltering Growth)

options.text: Chromosomal microarray and Fragile X analysis as first-line for syndromic short stature
options.rationale: Genetic testing is appropriate for proportionate short stature with dysmorphic features or developmental concern; this child has normal milestones and weight-led faltering growth, not syndromic short stature. (Source: RCH CPG: Faltering Growth)

options.text: Referral to a paediatric dietitian for food diary and high-energy supplement plan without further testing
options.rationale: Dietetic input is part of management once organic causes are excluded; commencing energy supplements before excluding coeliac or other malabsorption risks masking the diagnosis without resolving it. (Source: RCH CPG: Faltering Growth)

explanation.summary: Weight-led faltering growth in a toddler with preserved height and head circumference points to inadequate intake or malabsorption rather than endocrine or neurological pathology. The history of bulky pale stools and post-gluten bloating raises coeliac disease as the leading organic differential, requiring tissue transglutaminase IgA plus total IgA to avoid missing selective IgA deficiency. The first-line work-up also captures iron deficiency, renal causes, occult urinary infection, and thyroid dysfunction. Endocrine, genetic, and imaging investigations are reserved for short stature, dysmorphism, or neurological features.
key_points: Weight crossing two centiles down with bulky pale stools suggests malabsorption
Coeliac screen needs tissue transglutaminase IgA plus total IgA
Baseline FBC, ferritin, UEC, TSH and urine MCS capture common organic causes
Endocrine and genetic workup is for short stature or syndromic features, not weight-led faltering growth
explanation.pearls: Always order total IgA with tissue transglutaminase IgA; an isolated tissue transglutaminase IgA in IgA-deficient children is falsely negative and misses coeliac disease.

subtopic_detail: Bipolar I maintenance with depressive predominance
stem:
A 34-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder presents to your community psychiatry clinic 8 weeks after recovering from her most recent depressive episode. Her illness pattern over 6 years includes one hypomanic episode 5 years ago and four depressive episodes, the most recent requiring inpatient admission. She is currently euthymic and tolerated her acute treatment of quetiapine and sertraline well, but has gained 8 kg and complains of sedation.

Her renal function is normal. She is sexually active and uses a copper intrauterine device. She wants a single maintenance agent with the best evidence for preventing further depressive relapse.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 72, BP 124/78, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.5
data_table.Examination: Euthymic, no psychomotor change, no suicidal ideation; BMI 28.4 kg/m^2
data_table.Investigations: TSH 2.1; UEC normal; LFTs normal; eGFR > 90

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate maintenance pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Continue sertraline as the sole maintenance agent and review every 3 months in psychiatry clinic
options.rationale: Antidepressant monotherapy in bipolar I disorder raises the risk of mood switching and rapid cycling and is contraindicated as a maintenance strategy; an effective mood stabiliser is required. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

options.text: Switch to carbamazepine titrated to a target serum level for relapse prevention
options.rationale: Carbamazepine has efficacy in bipolar mania but is less effective than lamotrigine for preventing the depressive pole and carries significant CYP3A4 induction and rash burden. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Continue quetiapine 300 mg nocte indefinitely as the established maintenance choice in bipolar I disorder
options.rationale: Quetiapine has bipolar maintenance evidence but the patient has weight gain and sedation that are unlikely to remit; a switch to an agent with depressive-pole evidence and a better metabolic profile is appropriate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Initiate sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily and titrate to a maintenance serum trough level
options.rationale: Valproate is teratogenic and is not first-line for women of reproductive age; it also has weaker evidence than lamotrigine in preventing the depressive pole of bipolar I. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Initiate lamotrigine with slow titration to a target maintenance dose with monitoring for skin reaction
options.rationale: Lamotrigine has the strongest evidence for preventing the depressive pole of bipolar I disorder, is metabolically neutral, and is acceptable in women of reproductive age; slow titration over 6 weeks minimises the risk of serious cutaneous reaction. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG)

explanation.summary: Maintenance pharmacotherapy in bipolar I disorder is tailored to the predominant pole of relapse. Lithium remains gold standard for overall prevention but lamotrigine has the strongest evidence for preventing the depressive pole, with a clean metabolic and reproductive safety profile. Valproate is teratogenic and avoided in women of reproductive age. Antidepressant monotherapy is contraindicated due to manic switch risk. Quetiapine has maintenance evidence but its metabolic burden often drives a switch. Slow lamotrigine titration over 6 weeks reduces the risk of serious cutaneous adverse reactions.
key_points: Lamotrigine prevents depressive relapse in bipolar I with the strongest evidence base
Antidepressant monotherapy is unsafe in bipolar I disorder
Valproate is teratogenic and avoided in women of reproductive age
Lamotrigine titration is slow and the patient must report any new rash
explanation.pearls: Lamotrigine for the depressive pole, lithium for overall relapse prevention; combine when needed. Titrate lamotrigine over 6 weeks and stop on any rash.

subtopic_detail: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy vs HELLP
stem: A 34-year-old primigravida is admitted at 34+5 weeks gestation with 48 hours of progressive nausea, polydipsia, and right upper quadrant pain. She has had no headaches or visual disturbance. On arrival she is mildly jaundiced and lightly drowsy. Bloods on the labour ward triage show ALT 320, total bilirubin 60, INR 1.9, fibrinogen 1.4 g/L, platelets 110, creatinine 130, ammonia 80, capillary glucose 2.6 mmol/L, and lactate 4.2. The midwife notes the foetal heart rate is 150 bpm with reduced variability on the CTG.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 108, BP 138/86, SpO2 98% room air, Temp 36.6
data_table.Examination: Mild scleral icterus; tender right upper quadrant; no peripheral oedema; drowsy but rousable
data_table.Investigations: ALT 320, bilirubin 60, INR 1.9, fibrinogen 1.4 g/L, platelets 110, creatinine 130, ammonia 80, glucose 2.6 mmol/L, lactate 4.2

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Treat as severe HELLP with intravenous magnesium sulfate and expectant management until 36 weeks
options.rationale: Hypoglycaemia, hyperammonaemia, coagulopathy and lactic acidosis are not features of HELLP and point to acute fatty liver of pregnancy; expectant management is unsafe given progressive liver failure. (Source: RANZCOG: Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy

options.text: Stabilise with glucose, correct coagulopathy with fresh frozen plasma and cryoprecipitate, then proceed to expedited delivery
options.rationale: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is identified by Swansea criteria including encephalopathy, hypoglycaemia, hyperammonaemia and coagulopathy; treatment is rapid maternal stabilisation with dextrose and blood products followed by delivery, which is the only intervention that halts the disease. (Source: RANZCOG: Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy

options.text: Commence high-dose intravenous N-acetylcysteine as an antidote and admit to the obstetric ward for serial bloods
options.rationale: N-acetylcysteine has been used as an adjunct in some hepatic failure scenarios but is not the primary intervention in acute fatty liver of pregnancy, where delivery is curative. (Source: RANZCOG: Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy

options.text: Initiate urgent therapeutic plasma exchange and continue expectant management with twice daily CTG
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is reserved for refractory cases after delivery or for TTP; in acute fatty liver of pregnancy delivery itself reverses the underlying mitochondrial dysfunction. (Source: RANZCOG: Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy

options.text: Administer intravenous betamethasone for foetal lung maturation and plan delivery in 48 hours
options.rationale: Maternal coagulopathy and hepatic failure preclude delaying delivery for steroid cover; the neonatal team must manage prematurity after the immediate maternal threat is addressed. (Source: RANZCOG: Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy

explanation.summary: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a rare but life-threatening late-pregnancy disorder distinguished from HELLP by hypoglycaemia, hyperammonaemia, encephalopathy and a coagulopathy that often exceeds the platelet drop. The Swansea criteria provide a clinical framework with six or more of vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia, encephalopathy, raised bilirubin, hypoglycaemia, raised urate, leucocytosis, ascites or bright liver on ultrasound, raised transaminases, raised ammonia, raised creatinine, coagulopathy, and microvesicular steatosis on biopsy. Management is rapid correction of hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy and electrolytes followed by prompt delivery, which is the only definitive treatment.
key_points: Hypoglycaemia, hyperammonaemia, and coagulopathy distinguish AFLP from HELLP
Swansea criteria provide a structured diagnostic framework
Delivery is the only definitive treatment - do not delay for steroids
Stabilise with dextrose, FFP and cryoprecipitate before transferring to theatre
explanation.pearls: Profound hypoglycaemia and disproportionate coagulopathy in late pregnancy is AFLP, not HELLP. Steroid cover does not justify delaying delivery in this setting.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy
Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists Green-top Guideline 43: AFLP

stem: A 52-year-old woman with anti-acetylcholine receptor positive myasthenia gravis presents to the emergency department with worsening dyspnoea and dysphagia. She has had a vomiting illness for 36 hours and has missed her last three doses of pyridostigmine. On examination she has bilateral ptosis, a weak head lift, soft voice, paradoxical abdominal breathing, and weak cough. Her forced vital capacity at the bedside is 14 mL/kg and falling. She is alert and orientated but distressed.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110, RR 28 shallow, SpO2 96% on 4 L nasal prongs, BP 138/82, Temp 36.8
data_table.Examination: Bilateral ptosis; weak head lift; paradoxical abdominal breathing; weak cough; clear chest
data_table.Investigations: Bedside FVC 14 mL/kg; ABG: pH 7.32, PaCO2 52, PaO2 80, HCO3 26; CXR clear; CK normal

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g and admit to a high-dependency bed for monitoring
options.rationale: High-dose steroid alone risks a transient worsening of weakness and does not address the imminent respiratory failure; airway control must come first. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Increase the home pyridostigmine dose and treat the underlying vomiting with intravenous ondansetron
options.rationale: Excess pyridostigmine causes a cholinergic crisis with the same neuromuscular picture; in established myasthenic crisis cholinesterase inhibitors are typically withheld until immunotherapy takes effect. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Trial bilevel non-invasive ventilation overnight while continuing pyridostigmine at maintenance dose
options.rationale: Non-invasive ventilation may be considered in early bulbar-sparing crisis but is unsafe when bulbar weakness, weak cough and a falling FVC compromise secretion clearance and airway protection. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Intubate now with a non-depolarising relaxant avoided, then commence intravenous immunoglobulin or plasma exchange
options.rationale: Forced vital capacity at 14 mL/kg with rising PaCO2, bulbar weakness, and weak cough is myasthenic crisis; immediate intubation is required, succinylcholine and non-depolarising relaxants are avoided or carefully dose-adjusted, and definitive treatment is IVIG or plasma exchange. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Give intravenous edrophonium as a diagnostic challenge and titrate further therapy to the response
options.rationale: Edrophonium testing is no longer used in established myasthenia because of cardiac risk and adds no information when the diagnosis is already confirmed; the priority is airway control. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

explanation.summary: Myasthenic crisis is respiratory failure due to neuromuscular weakness and is precipitated by missed medication, infection, surgery, or new drugs that block neuromuscular transmission. A forced vital capacity below 15 mL/kg, weak cough, bulbar weakness, and rising PaCO2 are indications for intubation; do not rely on SpO2 because hypoxia is a late sign. Once airway is secured, definitive therapy is intravenous immunoglobulin or plasma exchange, with corticosteroids commenced under ventilatory cover because of the steroid dip. Pyridostigmine is usually withheld in crisis to avoid cholinergic crisis confusion and reinstated as bulbar function returns.
key_points: Intubate when FVC is below 15 mL/kg or bulbar weakness compromises the airway
Do not rely on SpO2; rising PaCO2 marks the failure
Definitive treatment is IVIG or plasma exchange
Withhold pyridostigmine during crisis to avoid cholinergic crisis overlap
explanation.pearls: The 20-30-40 rule flags impending failure: vital capacity below 20 mL/kg, inspiratory force less negative than -30, or expiratory force below 40 cmH2O. Falling FVC trumps SpO2 every time.

stem:
A 9-month-old previously well boy is brought to the emergency department with 12 hours of intermittent crying episodes during which he draws his knees to his chest and appears pale, followed by quiet drowsy periods. His mother reports one passage of stool that looked like raspberry jam this morning and two episodes of bilious vomiting. He has not fed since lunchtime.

On examination he is lethargic but rousable, with a sausage-shaped mass palpable in the right upper quadrant. There is no peritonitis. He weighs 9 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 158, RR 36, SpO2 98% room air, BP 90/55, Temp 37.2
data_table.Examination: Lethargic but rousable; CRT 3 s; soft abdomen with a sausage-shaped right upper quadrant mass; no peritonism
data_table.Investigations: Bedside USS: target sign in the right upper quadrant consistent with ileocolic intussusception

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intravenous fluid resuscitation and proceed to image-guided air enema reduction with surgical standby
options.rationale: Ileocolic intussusception without peritonitis is treated with hydrostatic or pneumatic enema reduction after fluid resuscitation; success rates exceed 80 percent and surgical standby is required for failed reduction or perforation. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Plan urgent laparotomy as first-line management because of bilious vomiting and the abdominal mass
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for failed enema reduction, peritonitis, perforation, or a pathological lead point; primary laparotomy is unnecessary and exposes the child to avoidable risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Administer ondansetron and observe in the short stay unit with serial abdominal examinations
options.rationale: Observation alone allows progression to bowel ischaemia and perforation; the child has confirmed ileocolic intussusception that requires active reduction. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone for likely bacterial gastroenteritis and reassess in 6 hours
options.rationale: Antibiotics are not indicated for intussusception; treating presumed gastroenteritis misses the diagnosis and delays reduction. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Perform a contrast meal and follow through study to confirm the diagnosis prior to definitive therapy
options.rationale: Ultrasound has confirmed the diagnosis; meal and follow through is not the diagnostic tool of choice and adds delay without changing management. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

explanation.summary: Ileocolic intussusception in infants between 3 months and 3 years presents with episodic colic, vomiting, redcurrant jelly stool, and a sausage-shaped abdominal mass. Bedside ultrasound showing a target or doughnut sign confirms the diagnosis. After fluid resuscitation, image-guided pneumatic or hydrostatic enema reduction in the radiology suite with surgical standby is the standard of care, with surgery reserved for failed reduction, peritonitis, perforation, or a pathological lead point. Bilious vomiting is common with prolonged intussusception and does not by itself mandate primary laparotomy.
key_points: Target sign on USS confirms ileocolic intussusception
First-line management is fluid resuscitation then pneumatic or hydrostatic enema reduction
Surgery is reserved for failed reduction, peritonitis, perforation, or a pathological lead point
Recurrence rate after enema reduction is 5 to 10 percent
explanation.pearls: Even infants with established bilious vomiting and lethargy can usually be reduced by air enema if there is no peritonism; primary laparotomy is the exception, not the rule.

stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 at 38+1 weeks gestation is in early labour at 4 cm dilation on the birth suite. Spontaneous rupture of membranes occurs and the midwife immediately calls you because the CTG drops from a baseline of 140 bpm to a sustained 70 bpm. On vaginal examination you palpate a pulsating loop of cord through the cervix. The presenting part is a high vertex. The woman is in pain but cooperative. The operating theatre is 3 minutes away and the anaesthetic team is on the unit.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal HR 92, BP 118/72, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.7
data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm dilated; pulsating cord palpable through the cervix; presenting part high vertex
data_table.Investigations: CTG: sustained foetal bradycardia 70 bpm; presentation vertex

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Apply gentle outward traction on the cord and place warm saline soaks until theatre is ready
options.rationale: Pulling on the cord and exposing it to atmospheric temperature provoke vasospasm and worsen foetal compromise; warm saline soaks are no longer recommended. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Insert an oxytocin infusion to expedite vaginal delivery in this multiparous woman with a vertex presentation
options.rationale: Augmentation traps the cord further against the presenting part and worsens foetal compromise; the goal is to relieve cord compression and deliver by caesarean unless vaginal delivery is imminent. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Place the woman in the knee-chest position with manual elevation of the presenting part and call category 1 caesarean
options.rationale: Manual elevation of the presenting part off the cord plus knee-chest or exaggerated Sims position relieves compression while the patient is transferred for category 1 caesarean section; tocolysis with terbutaline may be added if delivery is delayed. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Perform an immediate ventouse-assisted vaginal delivery as the head is engaged and the cervix is dilating
options.rationale: The cervix is only 4 cm dilated and the head is high; vacuum delivery is unsafe and inappropriate at this station and would delay definitive caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Fill the bladder with 500 mL of warm saline as the sole manoeuvre and prepare for elective caesarean
options.rationale: Bladder filling is a useful adjunct when transfer is prolonged, but manual elevation in the knee-chest position is the immediate first-line manoeuvre alongside an emergency category 1 call. (Source: RANZCOG: Cord Prolapse)

explanation.summary: Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency in which the foetal cord descends through the cervix with the presenting part still high, causing rapid foetal compromise through compression and vasospasm. Immediate manoeuvres are aimed at relieving compression: manual elevation of the presenting part by the examining hand, knee-chest or exaggerated Sims position, and where transfer is prolonged, bladder filling with 500 mL warm saline via a Foley catheter. Definitive management is category 1 caesarean section unless vaginal delivery is imminent. Avoid handling the cord and avoid exposing it to atmospheric temperature.
key_points: Manual elevation plus knee-chest or exaggerated Sims position relieves cord compression
Bladder filling is an adjunct when transfer to theatre is delayed
Category 1 caesarean is the definitive intervention
Do not handle or pull on the cord; avoid cooling and traction
explanation.pearls: Manual elevation by the examining hand remains the most effective immediate manoeuvre and stays in place until the abdomen is open. Knee-chest gravity-assists this.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline 50: Umbilical Cord Prolapse

stem: A 58-year-old woman with non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis attributable to childhood whooping cough presents to her general practice with 5 days of increased sputum volume, change to thicker yellow-green sputum, low-grade fever, and worsening exertional breathlessness compared with her usual baseline. Her routine sputum cultures over the past 2 years have grown Haemophilus influenzae only. She has not required hospital admission for 3 years and is otherwise well, taking azithromycin three times weekly as maintenance.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96, RR 22, SpO2 95% room air, BP 128/76, Temp 37.6
data_table.Examination: Coarse crackles at the right base; mild end-expiratory wheeze; no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: CXR ordered today; previous sputum cultures show Haemophilus influenzae; no Pseudomonas history

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in her antibiotic management?

options.text: Admit for intravenous ceftriaxone and switch to oral therapy after 48 hours of clinical improvement
options.rationale: Most non-Pseudomonas exacerbations of bronchiectasis are managed as out-patients with oral antibiotics for 14 days; intravenous therapy is reserved for systemic toxicity, hypoxia, or treatment failure. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Prescribe oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 5 days and review with sputum culture in 1 week
options.rationale: A 5 day course is insufficient for a bronchiectasis exacerbation; standard duration is 14 days to address the infected bronchial pool. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Continue azithromycin only, reinforce airway clearance technique, and review in 7 days
options.rationale: Maintenance azithromycin is for prevention and does not provide adequate cover for an acute exacerbation; an additional 14 day course of targeted antibiotic is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Commence nebulised tobramycin twice daily as monotherapy and review with sputum culture at 48 hours
options.rationale: Inhaled tobramycin is used for chronic Pseudomonas suppression, not for an acute Haemophilus exacerbation; an oral agent with Haemophilus cover is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

options.text: Send a sputum culture today and commence oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Australian guidance recommends a 14 day oral course directed at recent sputum isolates for a bronchiectasis exacerbation without sepsis; amoxicillin-clavulanate covers Haemophilus influenzae including beta-lactamase producers and concurrent sputum culture refines therapy if treatment fails. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

explanation.summary: Acute exacerbations of bronchiectasis are defined by an increase in sputum volume or purulence, worsening breathlessness, fever, or constitutional symptoms. Out-patient management is appropriate in the absence of sepsis, hypoxia, or treatment failure. Targeted oral antibiotics for 14 days are selected on the basis of the most recent sputum culture and known colonising organisms; in non-Pseudomonas patients with prior Haemophilus, amoxicillin-clavulanate provides reliable cover. Maintenance azithromycin reduces exacerbation frequency but does not treat an acute event. A fresh sputum culture is taken before treatment to refine therapy if symptoms do not resolve.
key_points: Bronchiectasis exacerbations are treated for 14 days, not 5 to 7 days
Antibiotic choice is guided by previous sputum culture
Maintenance azithromycin reduces exacerbation rate but does not cover acute infection
IV therapy is reserved for sepsis, hypoxia or treatment failure
explanation.pearls: Always send a sputum culture before starting empirical antibiotics in bronchiectasis to capture a possible new Pseudomonas isolate that changes both the agent and the duration.
